narrative: As a white male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting their likelihood of passing on the first try. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.70, LSAT score of 31, full-time student status, and school decile rankings from early years—and estimated a 57% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This prediction above 50% triggered the rejection of your admission, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to succeed based on historical patterns. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which is notably lower than the typical student's rank around the middle of their class and signaled weaker academic performance relative to peers. Your LSAT score, while on the lower side compared to the average of about 37, worked in your favor by nudging the prediction slightly toward passing, showing some potential in standardized testing. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA of 2.70, below the average of 3.21, provided a modest positive counterbalance but wasn't strong enough to offset the top concern. Overall, your situation stands out from typical admitted students, who tend to have higher decile rankings and stronger test scores that align with bar exam success rates. While your full-time enrollment matches the vast majority of students, it didn't sway the outcome much here. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a lawyer, but a data-driven flag on bar passage risk. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience. Many students in similar positions improve their profiles and gain admission elsewhere—persistence pays off. You might also explore schools with more holistic reviews or bar prep support programs tailored to students like you. Keep pushing; your story is far from over.
